1: Psalm 127, attributed to Solomon in its superscription, is the only wisdom psalm in the Songs of Ascents collection. Its placement is remarkable: the psalm about YHWH as the true builder of house and city is placed in the mouth of Solomon — the man who built both the temple and the royal palace. "Unless the LORD builds the house (yivneh bayit), the builders work to no purpose (shav amelu bonav bo)" — the house (bayit) encompasses both the physical dwelling and the household/family unit (the OT uses bayit for both). The claim is not that human building is irrelevant but that human building without divine initiative and support is ultimately purposeless. Solomon's temple was the greatest construction achievement of ancient Israel — and this psalm insists that it was YHWH who built it, not Solomon.
2: "It is pointless to rise before dawn and work until dark (meshane lahem), eating meals of anxious toil (lehem ha'atzavim) — for the LORD gives sleep as a gift to his beloved (shenah lididov)" — the lehem ha'atzavim (bread of anxious labor/pain) is the food produced by overwork driven by anxiety rather than trust — an echo of the Eden curse (Genesis 3:17-19: "by painful toil you will eat of it all the days of your life"). YHWH gives sleep as a gift to his yedid (beloved, the one he loves — the same word as Jedidiah, the covenant name given to Solomon in 2 Samuel 12:25). The gift of sleep signifies that the covenant person does not need to anxiously overwork because YHWH is working while they sleep. This is the theology of Sabbath applied to daily life: the day of rest is possible because YHWH governs and provides even when human effort has stopped.
3: "Yes, children are a gift from the LORD (nahalat YHWH); they are a reward he gives (sakar)" — nahalah (inheritance, allotment, what is assigned by God) is the term for the tribal land grants in Joshua: children are a gift-allotment from YHWH in the same way the promised land was a gift-allotment. The transfer of meaning from territorial inheritance to children is significant: what YHWH gives as covenant gift in the land-dimension, he also gives in the family-dimension. Sakar (reward, wages, payment) complements nahalah: children are what is received as the fruit of one's covenantal life, as wages are received for work done. The combination of inheritance (unconditional gift) and reward (responsive to faithfulness) captures the dual character of covenant blessing.
4: "Children born in a man's prime (bene hane'urim) are like arrows in the hand of a soldier" — children born in a man's youth grow to adulthood while he is still vigorous, giving him strong adult sons at his side during his later years. The arrow image: arrows are weapons that extend a warrior's reach beyond his immediate physical range — they act at a distance. Adult sons extended the father's legal, economic, and social reach beyond his own person: they could testify on his behalf, conduct business, represent the household in multiple locations simultaneously. "In the hand of a soldier" — arrows in the hand are ready for immediate use; adult sons at the father's side are similarly available and capable.
5: "How blessed is the man who has his quiver full of them! He will not be put to shame when he faces his enemies at the city gate (basha'ar)" — the city gate was the social, legal, and commercial center of ancient Israelite life: contracts were ratified there (Ruth 4:1-12), legal disputes were adjudicated there (Deuteronomy 21:19; Psalms 22:15; Psalms 25:7; Job 29:7; Psalms 31:21; Proverbs 31:23), and political decisions were announced there (2 Samuel 15:2; 2 Kings 7:1). To have "enemies at the gate" who challenge your legal standing, your debts, or your honor required witnesses and advocates. A man with many adult sons had multiple witnesses who could testify on his behalf and advocates who could press his legal case. The quiver full of arrows/sons meant legal security in the gate-court — the place where one's standing in the covenant community was publicly negotiated.
